session.user:
Update the following skills files to rename programmatic field names from "plan" to "pr". For each file, read it first, then apply edits.
**Rename patterns:** 1. `plan_id` â†’ `pr_id` (in code examples showing PlanRef fields, JSON fields) 2. `plan_url` â†’ `pr_url` (JSON field names) 3. `plan_number` â†’ `pr_number` (variable/JSON field names) 4. `numeric_plan_id` â†’ `numeric_pr_id` (property names in code examples) 5. `"Non-numeric plan_id"` â†’ `"Non-numeric pr_id"` (log messages) 6. `CreatePlanResult(plan_id=...)` â†’ `CreatePlanResult(pr_id=...)` (constructor calls) 7. `result.plan_id` â†’ `result.pr_id` (attribute access)
**DO NOT rename:** - "plan" as concept noun, class names like `PlanRef`, `CreatePlanResult`, `PlanNotFound` - `plan.md` references, skill names - `plan_backend` field name - The rename-swarm SKILL.md example that says "renaming `issue_number` to `plan_number`" â€” update this example to say "renaming `issue_number` to `pr_number`" since that's the current rename
**Files:**
1. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/erk-planning/references/workflow.md` â€” has `plan_id`, `plan_url` in JSON 2. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/dignified-python/dignified-python-core.md` â€” has `plan_id` in code examples (PlanRef class examples) 3. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/gateway-architecture.md` â€” has `CreatePlanResult(plan_id=...)` 4. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/anti-patterns.md` â€” has `CreatePlanResult(plan_id=...)` and `result.plan_id` 5. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/rename-swarm/SKILL.md` â€” has example text `issue_number` to `plan_number`, update to `pr_number` 6. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/refac-cli-push-down/SKILL.md` â€” has `plan_number` in JSON example
Read each file, make targeted edits.
session.assistant: I'll read and update each of these files to rename the programmatic field names from "plan" to "pr" as specified. Let me start by reading all the files.
session.tool_result:
1â†’# Plan Update Workflow 2â†’ 3â†’This document describes the complete workflow for updating an existing plan. 4â†’ 5â†’## When to Update vs Create New 6â†’ 7â†’| Scenario | Action | Rationale | 8â†’| ----------------------------- | ---------------------- | ------------------------- | 9â†’| Typos, clarifications | Update | Preserves PR history | 10â†’| Adding implementation details | Update | Same plan, more context | 11â†’| Plan fundamentally wrong | `/erk:replan` | Creates new with analysis | 12â†’| Major scope change | Create new, close old | Different work item | 13â†’| Plan partially implemented | Update remaining steps | Continue tracking | 14â†’ 15â†’## Update Workflow Steps 16â†’ 17â†’### Step 1: Get the Existing Plan Content 18â†’ 19â†’If you don't have the plan content locally, fetch it from GitHub: 20â†’ 21â†’```bash 22â†’# Get plan with comments 23â†’gh pr view 123 --comments --json comments,body 24â†’ 25â†’# The plan content is in the first comment's plan-body block 26â†’``` 27â†’ 28â†’Parse the first comment to extract content between: 29â†’ 30â†’```
... [96 lines omitted] ...
87â†’## Error Handling 89â†’| Error | Cause | Solution |

1â†’--- 2â†’--- 3â†’ 4â†’# Dignified Python - Core Standards 5â†’ 6â†’This document contains the core Python coding standards that apply to 80%+ of Python code. These principles are loaded with every skill invocation. 7â†’ 8â†’For conditional loading of specialized patterns: 9â†’ 10â†’- CLI development -> Load `cli-patterns.md` 11â†’- Subprocess operations -> Load `subprocess.md` 12â†’ 13â†’For detailed reference material, see the "When to Read Each Reference" section in SKILL.md. 14â†’ 15â†’--- 16â†’ 17â†’## The Cornerstone: LBYL Over EAFP 18â†’ 19â†’**Look Before You Leap: Check conditions proactively, NEVER use exceptions for control flow.** 20â†’ 21â†’This is the single most important rule in dignified Python. Every pattern below flows from this principle. 22â†’ 23â†’```python 24â†’# CORRECT: Check first 25â†’if key in mapping: 26â†’ value = mapping[key] 27â†’ process(value) 28â†’ 29â†’# WRONG: Exception as control flow 30â†’try:
... [502 lines omitted] ...
33â†’except KeyError: 39â†’## Exception Handling Basics 45â†’LBYL means checking conditions before acting. EAFP (Easier to Ask for Forgiveness than Permission) means trying operations and catching exceptions. In dignified Python, we strongly prefer LBYL. 65â†’# WRONG: KeyError as control flow 68â†’except KeyError: 72â†’### When Exceptions ARE Acceptable 74â†’Exceptions are ONLY acceptable at: 76â†’1. **Error boundaries** (CLI/API level) 80â†’**Default: Let exceptions bubble up** 82â†’**When you must use try-except: scope the try block to the single operation that raises.** Don't wrap multiple operations in one try block â€” each handler should know exactly what failed. Use early returns to keep subsequent code flat. 84â†’For detailed exception handling patterns including minimal exception scope, B904 chaining, third-party API examples, and anti-patterns, see `references/exception-handling.md`. 118â†’- `.resolve()` raises `OSError` for non-existent paths 119â†’- `.is_relative_to()` raises `ValueError` for invalid comparisons 120â†’- Checking `.exists()` first avoids exceptions entirely (LBYL!) 135â†’# WRONG: Using exceptions for path validation 140â†’except (OSError, ValueError): 180â†’3. **Inline imports only for specific exceptions** (circular deps, TYPE_CHECKING, conditional features) 211â†’**Acceptable exceptions:** 480â†’ log.warning("Non-numeric plan_id") 494â†’- **Raise exception**: When invalid data indicates a programming error or corrupt state 521â†’- **Exception chaining (B904), minimal exception scope**: `references/exception-handling.md`

1â†’--- 2â†’name: fake-driven-testing-gateway-architecture 3â†’description: Gateway layer architecture and interface patterns 4â†’--- 5â†’ 6â†’# Gateway Layer Architecture 7â†’ 8â†’**Read this when**: You need to understand or modify the gateway layer (the thin wrapper interfaces over external state). 9â†’ 10â†’## Overview 11â†’ 12â†’**Naming note**: "Gateway" is a common name for this pattern. These classes are also called **adapters**, **providers**, or **ports** in other contexts. The pattern matters more than the name. 13â†’ 14â†’## What Are Gateway Classes? 15â†’ 16â†’**Gateway classes are thin wrappers around heavyweight external APIs** that: 17â†’ 18â†’- Touch external state (filesystem, database, APIs, message queues) 19â†’- Could be slow (network calls, disk I/O, subprocess execution) 20â†’- Could fail periodically (network issues, rate limits, service outages) 21â†’- Are difficult to test directly 22â†’ 23â†’## The Four Implementations 24â†’ 25â†’Every gateway interface has **four implementations**: 26â†’ 27â†’### 1. Abstract Interface (ABC) 28â†’ 29â†’Defines the contract all implementations must follow. 30â†’
... [543 lines omitted] ...
113â†’ except Exception: 125â†’- Lets exceptions bubble up (no try/except for control flow) 158â†’ # Simulate failure if configured 160â†’ raise RuntimeError(f"Simulated failure for: {sql}") 344â†’ raise FileNotFoundError(f"File not found: {path}") 363â†’ raise FileNotFoundError(f"File not found: {path}") 494â†’ raise FileNotFoundError(f"File not found: {path}") 497â†’**Exception**: Fakes may create real directories when necessary for integration, but should not read/write actual files.

1â†’--- 2â†’name: rename-swarm 3â†’description: Parallel bulk rename across many files using a swarm of haiku agents. Use when mechanically renaming identifiers, parameters, or keys across 5+ files. 4â†’--- 5â†’ 6â†’You are an expert at orchestrating parallel mechanical renames across large codebases using swarms of lightweight agents. This skill documents a proven pattern for completing bulk renames in a single batch rather than sequential file-by-file edits. 7â†’ 8â†’## When to Use 9â†’ 10â†’- Renaming an identifier, parameter, key, or variable across **5+ files** 11â†’- The renames are **mechanical** â€” same find-and-replace logic in each file, no reasoning required 12â†’- Files are **independent** â€” editing file A doesn't affect what needs to change in file B 13â†’- Examples: renaming `issue_number` to `plan_number`, renaming `old_func` to `new_func`, updating a key name across config consumers 14â†’ 15â†’## When NOT to Use 16â†’ 17â†’- **Cross-file cascading refactors** where renaming a shared type changes method signatures, requiring each file to adapt differently 18â†’- **Renames requiring judgment** â€” e.g., "rename this concept" where each call site needs context-aware naming 19â†’- **Fewer than 5 files** â€” sequential edits are simpler and have less overhead 20â†’- **Complex AST transforms** â€” use `libcst-refactor` agent instead 21â†’ 22â†’## The Pattern 23â†’ 24â†’### Step 1: Identify All Files 25â†’ 26â†’Use Grep to find every file containing the target identifier: 27â†’ 28â†’``` 29â†’Grep(pattern="old_name", output_mode="files_with_matches") 30â†’```
... [102 lines omitted] ...
54â†’Collect results from all source file agents. Review for errors. 64â†’1. **Grep check**: Confirm old name no longer appears (except intentional exceptions) 98â†’If there are no exceptions, state: `"No exceptions â€” rename ALL occurrences."` 114â†’- **One agent per file** â€” keeps prompts focused, avoids cross-file edit conflicts, makes failures isolated and retriable.

1â†’--- 2â†’name: refac-cli-push-down 3â†’description: Moving mechanical computation from LLM prompts into tested CLI commands. Use when writing or reviewing slash commands with embedded bash, when a skill/command exceeds ~100 lines of procedural steps, when adding parsing/validation/transformation logic to markdown prompts, when debugging flaky embedded scripts, or when refactoring commands to reduce token overhead. 4â†’--- 5â†’ 6â†’You are an expert at identifying mechanical computation embedded in agent prompts and pushing it down into tested CLI commands. This skill documents a proven pattern for reducing prompt complexity, improving reliability, and making agent workflows testable. 7â†’ 8â†’## The Pattern 9â†’ 10â†’**If it requires understanding meaning, keep it in the agent. If it's mechanical transformation, push it to a CLI command.** 11â†’ 12â†’Like database query optimizers that "push down" predicates closer to the data layer for efficiency, this pattern moves computation from LLM prompts to tested code where it belongs. 13â†’ 14â†’| Push to CLI Command | Keep in Agent | 15â†’| --------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | 16â†’| Parsing/validation (URLs, formats, paths) | Semantic analysis (summarizing, naming) | 17â†’| Data extraction (JSON/YAML, filtering) | Content generation (docs, code, messages) | 18â†’| Deterministic operations (file queries, transforms) | Complex reasoning (trade-offs, ambiguity) | 19â†’| Token reduction (compressing, pre-filtering) | Decision-making (planning, interpretation) | 20â†’ 21â†’## Why It Matters 22â†’ 23â†’**Token reduction.** Every line of bash, parsing logic, and error handling in a markdown prompt consumes context tokens. Pushing mechanical work to code shrinks prompts by 50-70%, leaving the agent focused on decisions that require judgment. 24â†’ 25â†’**Testability.** You cannot unit test bash embedded in markdown. A CLI command is a regular function with pytest coverage, mocked dependencies, and edge case validation. Bugs get caught in CI, not during agent runs. 26â†’ 27â†’**Reliability.** Embedded bash varies between shells, lacks type safety, and fails silently on typos. A proper language provides a real execution environment with proper error handling, structured JSON output, and deterministic behavior. 28â†’ 29â†’**Composability.** Once logic lives in a CLI command, multiple skills and commands can invoke it. Changes to the logic propagate to all callers without updating markdown files. 30â†’
... [190 lines omitted] ...
50â†’ - On failure, include `error` with human-readable `message` 66â†’4. **Write tests first.** Create a test file alongside or in a parallel test directory. Test the happy path, error cases, and edge cases. Mock external dependencies (API calls, file system, subprocess). 132â†’7. If any check fails, list all failures with field paths 146â†’- On failure: `{"success": false, "errors": [{"field": "database.port", "message": "..."}]}` 148â†’If validation fails, report the errors to the user. If it succeeds, proceed with the resolved config. 151â†’Eight steps of deterministic validation collapsed into one call. The agent only handles the response â€” reporting errors or proceeding. 210â†’- **Structured JSON output** with a consistent contract (`success`, `error`, payload fields)

1â†’--- 2â†’name: fake-driven-testing-anti-patterns 3â†’description: Common testing mistakes to avoid 4â†’--- 5â†’ 6â†’# Testing Anti-Patterns 7â†’ 8â†’**Read this when**: You're unsure if your approach is correct, or want to avoid common mistakes. 9â†’ 10â†’## Overview 11â†’ 12â†’This document covers common anti-patterns in Python testing and how to avoid them. Each anti-pattern includes examples of what NOT to do and the correct approach. 13â†’ 14â†’## âťŚ Testing Speculative Features 15â†’ 16â†’**NEVER write tests for code that doesn't exist yet** (unless doing TDD RIGHT NOW). 17â†’ 18â†’### Wrong Approach 19â†’ 20â†’```python 21â†’# âťŚ WRONG: Placeholder test for future feature 22â†’# def test_feature_we_might_add_later(): 23â†’# """TODO: Implement this feature next sprint.""" 24â†’# pass 25â†’ 26â†’# âťŚ WRONG: Test stub for "maybe someday" idea 27â†’# def test_hypothetical_feature(): 28â†’# """Feature we're considering for Q2.""" 29â†’# # Not implemented yet, just a placeholder 30â†’# pass
... [809 lines omitted] ...
194â†’4. **CI/CD failures**: Paths may not exist on CI systems 195â†’5. **Permission errors**: Tests may not have write access to hardcoded paths 225â†’ # Old signature - type error! 231â†’ # Old signature - type error! 234â†’# Result: Type errors, broken tests, runtime failures 256â†’- **Type errors**: Implementations don't match interface 257â†’- **Runtime errors**: Tests pass locally but fail in production 295â†’# âś… CORRECT: Fast, better error messages (for Click CLIs) 329â†’- subprocess: Harder to set breakpoints, unclear errors 330â†’- Test clients: Direct access to exceptions, clear stack traces 525â†’### Exception: Directory Creation 604â†’ with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="Invalid order"): 819â†’| Not updating all layers | Type errors, broken tests | Update ABC/Real/Fake/DryRun |

tool_use.param: **Bad:** `{"plan_number": 42}` â€” agent must now fetch plan details separately. **Good:** `{"plan_number": 42, "title": "...", "phases": [...], "related_docs": {...}}` â€” agent can proceed immediately.

session.assistant:
Perfect! I've completed all the requested edits. Here's a summary of the changes made:
## Summary of Changes
I've successfully updated 6 skills files to rename programmatic field names from "plan" to "pr". All changes were targeted and surgical:
### 1. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/erk-planning/references/workflow.md` - Renamed JSON fields: `plan_id` â†’ `pr_id`, `plan_url` â†’ `pr_url`
### 2. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/dignified-python/dignified-python-core.md` - Updated PlanRef example: `plan_id: int` â†’ `pr_id: int` with matching constructor call - Updated anti-pattern example: `plan_id: str` â†’ `pr_id: str` - Renamed property: `numeric_plan_id` â†’ `numeric_pr_id` - Updated log message: `"Non-numeric plan_id"` â†’ `"Non-numeric pr_id"`
### 3. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/gateway-architecture.md` - Updated CreatePlanResult constructor: `CreatePlanResult(plan_id=...)` â†’ `CreatePlanResult(pr_id=...)`
### 4. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/fake-driven-testing/references/anti-patterns.md` - Updated attribute access: `result.plan_id` â†’ `result.pr_id`
### 5. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/rename-swarm/SKILL.md` - Updated example heading and description: `issue_number` to `plan_number` â†’ `issue_number` to `pr_number`
### 6. `/home/runner/work/erk/erk/.claude/skills/refac-cli-push-down/SKILL.md` - Updated JSON examples: `{"plan_number": 42}` â†’ `{"pr_number": 42}` and matching "good" example
All edits preserved class names, concept nouns (PlanRef, CreatePlanResult), and other non-field elements as specified. The renaming is now consistent across all skill documentation files.
